sections.content: What You'll Build

You will construct a fine-tuned Large Language Model specifically optimized for cricket domain tasks. This project demonstrates how transformers can be adapted for specialized vocabulary and context through instruction tuning and parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA). You'll build a system that understands cricket terminology, match statistics, player performance analysis, and strategic commentary generation. The model will handle cricket-specific named entities (player names, team abbreviations, venue names) and produce contextually accurate outputs using domain-specific prompts. By the end, you'll have a working pipeline that accepts cricket match queries and generates informed analytical responses. This hands-on exercise teaches you transfer learning in LLMs—how pre-trained models acquire new domain knowledge through minimal additional training data. You'll implement tokenizer customization, prompt engineering templates, and evaluation metrics tailored to cricket domain accuracy.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: When Rohit Sharma enters an innings, he doesn't rebuild his batting technique from scratch—he applies his foundational skills developed through years of training while adapting his approach to the specific conditions of the pitch, opposition bowling attack, and match situation. Similarly, your base LLM (like GPT-2 or Llama) arrives with general language knowledge from massive pre-training, but to make it excel at cricket analysis, you fine-tune it—much like how Sharma adjusts his strike rotation against spin bowlers in powerplay overs versus death bowling. The foundation (pre-trained weights) stays intact, but you're selectively reinforcing the model's understanding of cricket-specific patterns: how run rates accelerate, why certain batsmen dominate left-arm pace, what makes a successful Duckworth-Lewis chase strategy. Each cricket-specific example you feed during fine-tuning acts like a practice session where the model refines its internal representations of cricket concepts. This is parameter-efficient fine-tuning—you're not retraining the entire 7-billion parameter model, just adding a small LoRA adapter (like adding specialized coaching for one specific skill) rather than changing every single neuron. Understanding this parallel reveals why fine-tuning is so powerful: you get domain expertise without the computational cost of training from scratch, just as elite cricketers develop match-specific adaptations while retaining their core technique.
Prerequisites

items: Proficiency with Python 3.9+, including dataclasses, context managers, and JSON handling for configuration and match data
Understanding of transformer architecture: attention mechanisms, tokenization, embeddings, and the conceptual difference between pre-training and fine-tuning
Familiarity with PyTorch fundamentals: tensor operations, autograd, DataLoader construction, and custom loss functions for specialized training objectives
Knowledge of HuggingFace transformers library: loading pre-trained models, working with tokenizers, and using Trainer API for controlled model updates
Basic cricket domain knowledge: understanding match formats (T20, ODI, Test), batting/bowling terminology, key statistical metrics, and players from major international teams

sections.content: Setup & Project Structure

Set up a project directory called cricket_llm_tuner that organizes code, data, and models following MLOps conventions. Install PyTorch (with CUDA support if available), HuggingFace transformers, datasets library, and PEFT for parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Create subdirectories for raw cricket match data, processed datasets in parquet format, model checkpoints, and evaluation results. Use a Python virtual environment to isolate dependencies. Configure a constants file for model names, learning rates, batch sizes, and cricket-specific prompt templates. This structure supports reproducibility and scaling to larger training runs. The project uses a base model like GPT-2 or Llama-2-7b-hf and adds LoRA adapters for cricket domain specialization. Dataset includes match summaries, player statistics, and analytical questions with expected outputs.

#!/bin/bash
# Setup cricket_llm_tuner project structure

mkdir -p cricket_llm_tuner
cd cricket_llm_tuner

# Create subdirectories
mkdir -p data/raw data/processed
mkdir -p models/checkpoints models/adapters
mkdir -p results/evaluations results/outputs
mkdir -p notebooks logs

# Create Python virtual environment
python3.10 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate

# Upgrade pip and install core dependencies
pip install --upgrade pip setuptools wheel

# Install ML/AI stack
pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu118
pip install transformers==4.36.0 datasets==2.16.0 peft==0.7.0
pip install accelerate==0.25.0 bitsandbytes==0.41.0 wandb==0.16.0
pip install tqdm numpy pandas scikit-learn matplotlib seaborn

# Create project configuration files
cat > config.py << 'EOF'
# Configuration for cricket_llm_tuner

MODEL_NAME = "gpt2"  # or "meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf" for stronger base
LORA_R = 16
LORA_ALPHA = 32
LORA_DROPOUT = 0.05
LORA_TARGET_MODULES = ["c_attn"]  # GPT-2 attention modules

BATCH_SIZE = 4
GRADIENT_ACCUMULATION_STEPS = 4
LEARNING_RATE = 2e-4
NUM_EPOCHS = 3
WARMUP_STEPS = 100
MAX_SEQ_LENGTH = 512

CRICKET_TERMS = {
    "formats": ["T20", "ODI", "Test", "Domestic T20"],
    "positions": ["Opener", "Middle-order", "Lower-order", "Bowler"],
    "delivery_types": ["Pace", "Spin", "Off-break", "Leg-break", "Googly"],
}
EOF

echo "✓ Project structure created successfully"
echo "✓ Virtual environment ready: source venv/bin/activate"
echo "✓ Dependencies installed"
ls -la
Step 1 — Foundation: Cricket Domain Dataset & Tokenizer Configuration

Step 1 establishes the data pipeline and custom tokenizer configuration. Create a cricket match dataset containing real match summaries, player statistics, and analytical Q&A pairs sourced from IPL, international cricket databases. Build a custom tokenizer that recognizes cricket-specific tokens (player names, team abbreviations like CSK, MI, RCB, venues like MCG) as atomic units to prevent subword fragmentation. Implement data preprocessing that normalizes match records, handles missing statistics, and creates prompt-response pairs following instruction-tuning formats. The tokenizer will map cricket entities correctly—'Virat Kohli' remains one token, not split into subwords, improving downstream model understanding. Create data loaders that batch examples efficiently while respecting sequence length constraints. This foundation ensures the model receives high-quality, domain-aware training signals from the very first epoch.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Before a Test match at Lord's, the England cricket team conducts a detailed preparation strategy—they study detailed match statistics, current weather patterns specific to the venue, recent performance data of Indian players against English conditions, and they assign specific fielding stations based on historical ball-tracking data. Your tokenizer customization is exactly this strategic preparation. Just as the team recognizes that 'Joe Root's technique against Jasprit Bumrah's back-of-length yorker' is a specific match scenario requiring targeted preparation (not generic batting practice), your custom tokenizer ensures 'Jasprit Bumrah' stays intact rather than being fragmented into 'J' + 'asp' + 'rit' + 'Bumrah'. The cricket dataset you curate acts like the match video library—centuries of innings footage analyzed to extract patterns about run rates, wicket probabilities, and successful strategies. When the model trains on this dataset during fine-tuning, it's absorbing cricket-specific contextual understanding, much like how Bumrah's economy figures improve through analyzing batsmen's weaknesses. The preprocessing steps (normalizing statistics formats, handling missing DRS review data) are like ensuring all match records are in standardized scorecard format before analysis. This preparation phase determines whether the model will understand that an economy of 6.5 in powerplay is excellent while 8.2 in death overs is poor—contextual knowledge that only emerges through domain-aware data engineering.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Step 1: Cricket Domain Dataset & Tokenizer Configuration
"""

import json
import os
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List, Dict, Tuple
from pathlib import Path

import torch
from transformers import GPT2Tokenizer, PreTrainedTokenizer
from datasets import Dataset, DatasetDict
import pandas as pd


@dataclass
class CricketMatch:
    """Represents a cricket match with metadata"""
    match_id: str
    match_format: str  # T20, ODI, Test
    team_a: str
    team_b: str
    venue: str
    date: str
    winner: str
    winning_margin: str
    toss_winner: str
    toss_decision: str


@dataclass
class CricketPlayerRecord:
    """Individual player performance in a match"""
    player_name: str
    team: str
    role: str  # Batsman, Bowler, All-rounder
    runs_scored: int = 0
    balls_faced: int = 0
    wickets_taken: int = 0
    overs_bowled: float = 0
    economy_rate: float = 0.0
    strike_rate: float = 0.0


class CricketTokenizer:
    """Custom tokenizer that preserves cricket domain entities"""

    def __init__(self
sections.model_name:
str = "gpt2"):
        self.base_tokenizer = GPT2Tokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
        self.cricket_entities = self._build_cricket_vocab()
        self._add_special_tokens()

    def _build_cricket_vocab(self) -> Dict[str, int]:
        """Build cricket-specific vocabulary to prevent subword splitting"""
        entities = {
            "players": [
                "Virat Kohli", "Rohit Sharma", "Jasprit Bumrah",
                "AB de Villiers", "Steve Smith", "Kane Williamson",
                "Babar Azam", "Pat Cummins", "Mitchell Starc",
                "Rashid Khan", "Yuzvendra Chahal", "Mohammed Siraj"
            ],
            "teams": [
                "RCB", "MI", "CSK", "DC", "KKR", "PBKS", "RR", "SRH",
                "India", "Australia", "England", "Pakistan", "West Indies",
                "New Zealand", "Sri Lanka", "South Africa"
            ],
            "venues": [
                "MCG", "Sydney Cricket Ground", "Lord's",
                "Eden Gardens", "Wankhede Stadium", "Feroz Shah Kotla",
                "GABBA", "Old Trafford", "Trent Bridge"
            ],
            "statistics": [
                "economy rate", "strike rate", "average",
                "run rate", "required run rate", "Duckworth-Lewis",
                "maiden over", "death overs", "powerplay"
            ],
            "roles": [
                "Opener", "Middle-order", "Lower-order",
                "Pace bowler", "Spinner", "All-rounder"
            ]
        }

        vocab = {}
        token_id = self.base_tokenizer.vocab_size

        for category, terms in entities.items():
            for term in terms:
                vocab[term] = token_id
                token_id += 1

        return vocab

    def _add_special_tokens(self):
        """Add cricket-specific special tokens"""
        special_tokens = {
            "additional_special_tokens": [
                "<MATCH_START>", "<MATCH_END>",
                "<INNINGS_1>", "<INNINGS_2>",
                "<CRICKET_STATS>", "</CRICKET_STATS>",
                "<PLAYER>", "</PLAYER>"
            ]
        }
        self.base_tokenizer.add_special_tokens(special_tokens)

    def tokenize(self
sections.text:
str) -> List[str]:
        """Tokenize while preserving cricket entities"""
        for entity in self.cricket_entities.keys():
            # Placeholder: in production, use regex-based entity recognition
            pass
        return self.base_tokenizer.tokenize(text)

    def encode(self

sections.max_length:
int = 512) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Encode text to token IDs with padding"""
        encoding = self.base_tokenizer(
            text,
            max_length=max_length,
            padding="max_length",
            truncation=True,
            return_tensors="pt"
        )
        return encoding["input_ids"]


class CricketDatasetBuilder:
    """Build training dataset from cricket match records"""

    def __init__(self
sections.tokenizer:
CricketTokenizer):
        self.tokenizer = tokenizer
        self.matches: List[CricketMatch] = []
        self.player_records: List[CricketPlayerRecord] = []

    def add_match_record(self
sections.match:
CricketMatch):
        """Add a cricket match to the dataset"""
        self.matches.append(match)

    def add_player_record(self
sections.record:
CricketPlayerRecord):
        """Add individual player performance"""
        self.player_records.append(record)

    def create_instruction_tuning_examples(self) -> List[Dict]:
        """Create instruction-response pairs for fine-tuning"""
        examples = []

        # Example 1: Match analysis
        examples.append({
            "instruction": "Analyze the match performance",
            "input": (
                "Match: RCB vs MI at Wankhede Stadium. "
                "RCB scored 180/5. MI won by 6 runs. "
                "Virat Kohli scored 45 off 32 balls. "
                "Jasprit Bumrah took 2/38 from 4 overs."
            ),
            "output": (
                "Virat Kohli's strike rate of 140.6 was aggressive in powerplay. "
                "Jasprit Bumrah's economy of 9.5 suggests MI's middle overs were expensive. "
                "RCB's total of 180 was competitive but fell short by 6 runs on Wankhede's smaller ground."
            )
        })

        # Example 2: Player performance
        examples.append({
            "instruction": "Evaluate player form",
            "input": (
                "Rohit Sharma: 52 runs off 38 balls in last 3 T20 matches. "
                "Strike rate: 136.8. Average: 34.0"
            ),
            "output": (
                "Rohit Sharma is in excellent form with a strike rate above 130. "
                "His average of 34 across T20s shows consistent batting at the top of the order. "
                "The balance between aggression and stability makes him ideal for powerplay batting."
            )
        })

        # Example 3: Bowling strategy
        examples.append({
            "instruction": "Suggest bowling strategy",
            "input": (
                "Target: 165 in T20. Powerplay overs: 6. "
                "Current run rate: 8.2 per over. "
                "Batsmen: AB de Villiers (LHB, aggressive) and Steve Smith (RHB, technical)"
            ),
            "output": (
                "Deploy pace bowler with short-of-length line against Smith in powerplay. "
                "Use off-spinner to cramp De Villiers' room on the off side. "
                "Death bowling requires slower balls at yorker length. "
                "Target economy: under 7 runs per over in powerplay."
            )
        })

        return examples

    def build_dataset(self
sections.examples:
List[Dict]) -> Dataset:
        """Convert examples to HuggingFace Dataset"""
        def format_example(ex: Dict) -> Dict:
            prompt = f"""<MATCH_START>
Instruction: {ex['instruction']}
Input: {ex['input']}
<CRICKET_STATS>
Response: {ex['output']}
</CRICKET_STATS>
<MATCH_END>"""
            return {
                "text": prompt,
                "input_ids": self.tokenizer.encode(prompt).squeeze().tolist()
            }

        formatted = [format_example(ex) for ex in examples]
        return Dataset.from_dict({
            "text": [f["text"] for f in formatted],
            "input_ids": [f["input_ids"] for f in formatted]
        })


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Initialize
    cricket_tokenizer = CricketTokenizer(model_name="gpt2")
    dataset_builder = CricketDatasetBuilder(cricket_tokenizer)

    # Create sample matches
    sample_match = CricketMatch(
        match_id="IPL_2024_001",
        match_format="T20",
        team_a="RCB",
        team_b="MI",
        venue="Wankhede Stadium",
        date="2024-03-31",
        winner="MI",
        winning_margin="6 runs",
        toss_winner="RCB",
        toss_decision="bat"
    )

    dataset_builder.add_match_record(sample_match)

    # Create instruction-tuning examples
    examples = dataset_builder.create_instruction_tuning_examples()
    print(f"Created {len(examples)} instruction-tuning examples")

    # Build dataset
    train_dataset = dataset_builder.build_dataset(examples)
    print(f"\nDataset shape: {len(train_dataset)} examples")
    print(f"\nSample instruction-response pair:")
    print(f"Text: {train_dataset[0]['text'][:200]}...")

    # Save dataset
    os.makedirs("data/processed", exist_ok=True)
    train_dataset.save_to_disk("data/processed/cricket_instruction_tuning")
    print("\n✓ Dataset saved to data/processed/cricket_instruction_tuning")

sections.content: Step 2 — Core Logic: LoRA Fine-Tuning Pipeline

Step 2 implements the parameter-efficient fine-tuning using LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation). Instead of updating all model parameters (prohibitively expensive for 7B+ models), LoRA adds small trainable matrices (rank 16) to selected layers, reducing trainable parameters from billions to millions. Configure LoRA target modules (typically attention layers) where domain adaptation matters most. Initialize the trainer with appropriate learning rates, gradient accumulation, mixed precision training for memory efficiency, and distributed training support. Implement cricket-domain specific loss computation that may weight player names and statistics more heavily. Monitor training with loss curves and validation metrics. This approach achieves 95% of full fine-tuning quality with 5% computational cost, essential for resource-constrained environments. The LoRA matrices act as learned adapters that inject cricket knowledge into the frozen base model.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: When Jasprit Bumrah arrived in international cricket, the Indian team didn't rebuild him from scratch—he had fundamental bowling mechanics developed through years of domestic cricket. However, to make him exceptional in fast-paced T20 cricket against world-class batsmen, coaching staff added specialized training: yorker practice against specific fielding configurations, studying left-handed batsmen's weaknesses, death-over execution drills. This targeted coaching didn't change Bumrah's fundamental action (frozen base model), but added specific adaptations to maximize performance in high-pressure situations (cricket domain tasks). Similarly, LoRA fine-tuning keeps the entire pre-trained GPT-2 model frozen but adds small 'adapter modules' in attention layers—these are like Bumrah's specialized T20 coaching that doesn't alter his core technique. The rank-16 LoRA matrices are incredibly compact (like a focused 2-week training camp) compared to retraining his entire biomechanics (full model fine-tuning). When the model encounters a cricket question—'Who would be ideal for death-overs bowling in T20?'—the LoRA adapters activate, combining base language understanding with cricket-specialized reasoning just as Bumrah combines textbook fast-bowling mechanics with his learned variations. This efficiency explains why LoRA-adapted models perform so well: you're getting specialized expertise (cricket domain knowledge) without the computational cost of complete retraining, much like Bumrah's international success comes from selective expertise development on frozen fundamental skills.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Step 2: LoRA Fine-Tuning Pipeline for Cricket Domain LLM
"""

import os
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional, Tuple

import torch
from torch.optim import AdamW
from torch.optim.lr_scheduler import LinearLR
from transformers import (
    AutoModelForCausalLM,
    AutoTokenizer,
    Trainer,
    TrainingArguments,
    DataCollatorForLanguageModeling,
)
from datasets import load_from_disk
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, TaskType


@dataclass
class LoRAConfig:
    """Configuration for LoRA fine-tuning"""
    lora_r: int = 16
    lora_alpha: int = 32
    lora_dropout: float = 0.05
    lora_target_modules: list = None
    bias: str = "none"

    def __post_init__(self):
        if self.lora_target_modules is None:
            self.lora_target_modules = ["c_attn", "c_proj"]  # GPT-2 attention modules


@dataclass
class TrainingConfig:
    """Configuration for training run"""
    output_dir: str = "models/checkpoints"
    num_train_epochs: int = 3
    per_device_train_batch_size: int = 4
    per_device_eval_batch_size: int = 4
    gradient_accumulation_steps: int = 4
    learning_rate: float = 2e-4
    warmup_steps: int = 100
    max_grad_norm: float = 1.0
    weight_decay: float = 0.01
    logging_steps: int = 10
    eval_steps: int = 50
    save_steps: int = 50
    save_total_limit: int = 3
    fp16: bool = True
    gradient_checkpointing: bool = True
    dataloader_pin_memory: bool = True


class CricketLLMFineTuner:
    """Fine-tune LLM for cricket domain using LoRA"""

    def __init__(
        self,
        model_name: str = "gpt2",
        lora_config: LoRAConfig = None,
        training_config: TrainingConfig = None
    ):
        self.model_name = model_name
        self.lora_config = lora_config or LoRAConfig()
        self.training_config = training_config or TrainingConfig()
        self.model = None
        self.tokenizer = None
        self.trainer = None

    def load_base_model(self) -> Tuple[torch.nn.Module, AutoTokenizer]:
        """Load pre-trained model and tokenizer"""
        print(f"Loading base model: {self.model_name}")
        tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(self.model_name)
        tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

        model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
            self.model_name,
            torch_dtype=torch.float16 if self.training_config.fp16 else torch.float32,
            device_map="auto" if torch.cuda.is_available() else None
        )

        return model, tokenizer

    def apply_lora(self
sections.model:
torch.nn.Module) -> torch.nn.Module:
        """Apply LoRA adapters to the model"""
        print(f"Applying LoRA configuration...")
        print(f"  - Rank: {self.lora_config.lora_r}")
        print(f"  - Alpha: {self.lora_config.lora_alpha}")
        print(f"  - Target modules: {self.lora_config.lora_target_modules}")

        peft_config = LoraConfig(
            r=self.lora_config.lora_r,
            lora_alpha=self.lora_config.lora_alpha,
            lora_dropout=self.lora_config.lora_dropout,
            bias=self.lora_config.bias,
            task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM,
            target_modules=self.lora_config.lora_target_modules,
        )

        peft_model = get_peft_model(model, peft_config)
        peft_model.print_trainable_parameters()
        return peft_model

    def setup_training(
        self,
        train_dataset,
        eval_dataset=None,
        callbacks=None
    ) -> Trainer:
        """Initialize training infrastructure"""
        print("Setting up training pipeline...")

        training_args = TrainingArguments(
            output_dir=self.training_config.output_dir,
            num_train_epochs=self.training_config.num_train_epochs,
            per_device_train_batch_size=self.training_config.per_device_train_batch_size,
            per_device_eval_batch_size=self.training_config.per_device_eval_batch_size,
            gradient_accumulation_steps=self.training_config.gradient_accumulation_steps,
            learning_rate=self.training_config.learning_rate,
            warmup_steps=self.training_config.warmup_steps,
            max_grad_norm=self.training_config.max_grad_norm,
            weight_decay=self.training_config.weight_decay,
            logging_dir="logs",
            logging_steps=self.training_config.logging_steps,
            eval_strategy="steps" if eval_dataset else "no",
            eval_steps=self.training_config.eval_steps,
            save_strategy="steps",
            save_steps=self.training_config.save_steps,
            save_total_limit=self.training_config.save_total_limit,
            fp16=self.training_config.fp16,
            gradient_checkpointing=self.training_config.gradient_checkpointing,
            dataloader_pin_memory=self.training_config.dataloader_pin_memory,
            report_to=["tensorboard"],
            push_to_hub=False,
        )

        data_collator = DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(
            tokenizer=self.tokenizer,
            mlm=False
        )

        trainer = Trainer(
            model=self.model,
            args=training_args,
            train_dataset=train_dataset,
            eval_dataset=eval_dataset,
            data_collator=data_collator,
            callbacks=callbacks or [],
        )

        return trainer

    def train(
        self,
        train_dataset,
        eval_dataset=None,
        resume_from_checkpoint: Optional[str] = None
    ) -> dict:
        """Execute fine-tuning"""
        print("\n" + "="*60)
        print("Starting Cricket Domain LLM Fine-Tuning")
        print("="*60)

        # Load model and tokenizer
        self.model, self.tokenizer = self.load_base_model()

        # Apply LoRA
        self.model = self.apply_lora(self.model)

        # Setup training
        self.trainer = self.setup_training(train_dataset, eval_dataset)

        # Execute training
        print(f"\nTraining dataset size: {len(train_dataset)}")
        if eval_dataset:
            print(f"Evaluation dataset size: {len(eval_dataset)}")
        print(f"Total training steps: {len(self.trainer.get_train_dataloader()) // self.training_config.gradient_accumulation_steps * self.training_config.num_train_epochs}")
        print()

        train_result = self.trainer.train(resume_from_checkpoint=resume_from_checkpoint)

        return train_result.metrics

    def save_adapter(self
sections.adapter_path:
str = "models/adapters/cricket_lora"):
        """Save LoRA adapter weights"""
        os.makedirs(adapter_path, exist_ok=True)
        self.model.save_pretrained(adapter_path)
        print(f"\n✓ LoRA adapter saved to {adapter_path}")

    def save_model(self
sections.model_path:
str = "models/cricket_llm_finetuned"):
        """Merge adapter with base model and save"""
        os.makedirs(model_path, exist_ok=True)
        merged_model = self.model.merge_and_unload()
        merged_model.save_pretrained(model_path)
        self.tokenizer.save_pretrained(model_path)
        print(f"\n✓ Merged model saved to {model_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Load dataset
    print("Loading cricket instruction-tuning dataset...")
    train_dataset = load_from_disk("data/processed/cricket_instruction_tuning")
    print(f"Dataset loaded: {len(train_dataset)} examples")

    # Configure LoRA
    lora_config = LoRAConfig(
        lora_r=16,
        lora_alpha=32,
        lora_dropout=0.05,
        lora_target_modules=["c_attn", "c_proj"]
    )

    # Configure training
    training_config = TrainingConfig(
        output_dir="models/checkpoints",
        num_train_epochs=1,  # 1 epoch for demo
        per_device_train_batch_size=2,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=2,
        learning_rate=2e-4,
        warmup_steps=10,
        logging_steps=5,
        save_steps=10,
    )

    # Initialize and train
    finetuner = CricketLLMFineTuner(
        model_name="gpt2",
        lora_config=lora_config,
        training_config=training_config
    )

    metrics = finetuner.train(train_dataset)
    print("\n" + "="*60)
    print("Training Complete")
    print("="*60)
    print(f"Final loss: {metrics.get('train_loss', 'N/A'):.4f}")

    # Save trained adapter
    finetuner.save_adapter()
    print("\n✓ Cricket LLM fine-tuning pipeline completed!")

sections.content: Step 3 — Integration & Enhancement: Inference & Domain Evaluation

Step 3 brings the system to production-readiness by building an inference pipeline and cricket-domain-specific evaluation metrics. Load the fine-tuned LoRA adapter and base model for generation tasks. Implement prompt templates optimized for cricket queries: player performance analysis, match strategy recommendations, statistical comparisons. Create batch inference capabilities for processing multiple cricket questions efficiently. Build evaluation metrics beyond standard perplexity: measure factual accuracy of cricket statistics, appropriateness of strategic recommendations, and entity recognition accuracy for player/team names. Implement cricket knowledge grounding where responses are checked against a knowledge base of historical statistics. Add human evaluation guidelines for assessing response quality. The integration layer handles model quantization for faster inference, caching mechanism for repeated queries about same players/matches, and structured output parsing to extract actionable cricket insights.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: After months of intensive net practice, training camps, and LoRA-style specialized coaching, a cricketer is finally ready for a Test match at Eden Gardens. But readiness isn't just mechanical skill—the team needs to verify through warm-up matches that the player performs under real match pressure, against specific opposition strategies, with actual crowd noise. Your inference pipeline is this match-day validation. The fine-tuned cricket LLM must now prove itself on live cricket questions: 'Given Virat Kohli's recent form against spinners (statistical grounding), what's the optimal field placement at Lord's?' The model must access a knowledge base like a captain referencing historical scorecards, recalling that Kohli struggles with leg-spin around off-stump at that venue. Custom evaluation metrics are like post-match analysis: a 95% win probability prediction is worthless if the actual result contradicts historical trends, just as recommending an aggressive approach against quality pace bowling when defensive strategy won 8 of 10 similar matches is poor strategy. The human evaluation step—where domain experts like cricket analysts review responses—is exactly the captain's debrief after a match, validating that recommended strategies actually align with proven cricket tactics. Performance under production constraints (lower latency, smaller model size through quantization) mirrors a player adapting to different ground conditions—the fundamentals (LoRA-adapted knowledge) remain, but execution must suit the environment (inference hardware). This integration phase reveals whether your cricket domain adaptation was authentic learning versus statistical pattern-matching.

Dict) -> str:
        """Enhance response with knowledge base grounding"""
        grounded = response

        # Add player statistics if applicable
        for player in entities.get(CricketEntityType.PLAYER, []):
            if player in self.knowledge_base.player_stats:
                avg_t20 = self.knowledge_base.get_player_stat(player, "t20_average")
                if avg_t20:
                    grounded += f"\n[Knowledge Base: {player}'s T20 average is {avg_t20}]"

        return grounded

    def generate_response(
        self,
        query: str,
        task_type: str = "player_analysis",
        config: Optional[CricketGenerationConfig] = None
    ) -> Dict:
        """Generate cricket-domain response"""
        config = config or CricketGenerationConfig()

        # Format prompt
        prompt_template = self.CRICKET_PROMPTS.get(task_type, self.CRICKET_PROMPTS["player_analysis"])
        prompt = prompt_template.format(input=query)

        # Extract entities
        entities = self.extract_cricket_entities(query)

        # Generate
        print(f"Generating response for task: {task_type}")
        print(f"Query: {query}")
        print(f"Identified entities: {entities}")

        outputs = self.generator(
            prompt,
            max_length=config.max_length,
            temperature=config.temperature,
            top_p=config.top_p,
            repetition_penalty=config.repetition_penalty,
            num_return_sequences=1,
            do_sample=True,
            pad_token_id=self.tokenizer.eos_token_id
        )

        raw_response = outputs[0]["generated_text"]
        # Extract response after "Response:" marker
        response_start = raw_response.find("Response:") + len("Response:")
        response = raw_response[response_start:].strip()

        # Ground with knowledge base
        grounded_response = self.ground_response(response, entities)

        return {
            "query": query,
            "task_type": task_type,
            "response": response,
            "grounded_response": grounded_response,
            "entities": {
                k.value: v for k, v in entities.items()
            }
        }

sections.content: Step 4 — Testing & Verification

Execute the complete pipeline with test data to verify functionality at each stage. Run the dataset builder to confirm instruction-tuning examples format correctly. Execute a brief training run (1-2 epochs) on sample cricket data to validate the LoRA fine-tuning loop, loss computation, and checkpoint saving. Generate sample inference queries about famous cricket matches and players, confirming the model produces coherent cricket-domain responses. Verify entity extraction correctly identifies player names and teams. Check that knowledge base grounding enhances responses with factual cricket statistics. Validate that all save/load mechanisms work properly for model checkpoints and adapters.
